title: 37th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2021 — Junior (Grades 9-10)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

solutions.1: Kangaroo Day is the third Thursday of March each year. For 2024: March 1 falls on a Friday, so Thursdays are March 7, 14, 21 — the third Thursday is March 21, not March 14. All other dates are correct, so March 14, 2024 is the wrong date.
solutions.2: The temperatures for the five days are: Fri=-1°C, Sat=-2°C, Sun=0°C, Mon=6°C, Tue=2°C. The graph goes slightly down, then up to 0, then jumps to 6, then drops to 2 — this matches option B.
solutions.3: In an equilateral triangle park, path P goes directly along one side, while paths Q and R take zigzag routes of different lengths. By comparing the straight-line distance versus staircase paths, P is the shortest and Q is the longest, giving P < R < Q.
solutions.4: The top-left rectangle has height 6 cm and area 32 cm², so its width is 32/6. Working through the grid: each column width and row height can be determined from neighboring areas. The bottom-right rectangle's height works out to 5 cm.
solutions.5: At half-time: home 9, visiting 14. Let the visiting team score x goals in the second half; home scores 2x. Final: home = 9+2x, visiting = 14+x. Home wins by 1: 9+2x = 14+x+1, so x=6. Final score: home = 9+12=21, visiting = 14+6=20. Score: 21:20.
solutions.6: Each rhombus has area 5 cm², and 6 rhombuses form the star. The regular hexagon consists of the 6 rhombuses plus 6 equilateral triangles formed at the center. Each central triangle has area equal to half a rhombus = 2.5 cm². Total hexagon area = 6×5 + 6×2.5 = 30+15 = 45 cm².
solutions.7: The total age of all 6 members = 6×21 = 126. The three known members have ages 19+20+21 = 60. So Ahmed+Majed+Badr = 126−60 = 66. Since all three are the same age, each is 66÷3 = 22. Badr is 22 years old.
solutions.8: The full rectangle has perimeter 30 cm, so length+width = 15. One of the four sections is a 3×3 square (area=9 cm²). The sub-rectangle ABCD is the section diagonally opposite the square, with dimensions (w−3)×(h−3). Its perimeter = 2((w−3)+(h−3)) = 2(w+h−6) = 2(15−6) = 18 cm.
solutions.9: The two shown triangles include one right-angled isosceles and one right-angled non-isosceles. The third triangle must be isosceles but NOT right-angled, and must share the same area as one of the shown triangles. Option D provides an isosceles triangle with matching area that is not right-angled, satisfying all three constraints.
solutions.10: Let the number be x. Subtracting 1/10 gives x − 1/10; multiplying by 1/10 gives x/10. Setting equal: x − 1/10 = x/10. Then x − x/10 = 1/10, so 9x/10 = 1/10, giving x = 1/9.
solutions.11: Fahd lights each sparkler when the previous one is 1/10 remaining. Each sparkler burns for 2 minutes total. When sparkler k is lit, it has already 1/10 remaining = 0.2 min left, then sparkler k+1 burns fully (2 min), contributing 2 min. Total time = first sparkler fully burned (2 min) + 9 overlapping sections each contributing 2×(9/10) min... Summing: each sparkler after the first contributes 2×(9/10) = 1.8 min. Total = 2 + 9×1.8 = 2 + 16.2 = 18.2 min = 18 minutes 12 seconds.
solutions.12: Faisal climbs 8 steps, skipping step 6. He can only step on: 1,2,3,4,5,7,8. Count paths reaching step 8 without using step 6. Let f(n) = ways to reach step n. f(1)=1, f(2)=2, f(3)=3, f(4)=5, f(5)=8. Step 6 is blocked, so f(6)=0. f(7)=f(6)+f(5)=0+8=8. f(8)=f(7)+f(6)=8+0=8. Answer: 8 ways.
solutions.13: Numbers 1-6 are placed in the small circles at ring intersections. The sum of all numbers = 21. Three rings each have the same sum S. Number 6 is shown in a specific circle. Each intersection number is counted in two rings. Total across rings = 21 + (numbers at double intersections). With 6 placed as shown and working through the constraint that each ring has equal sum, the number in the question mark position is 1.
solutions.14: 2021 has remainder 5 when divided by 6, 7, 8, and 9. This means 2021−5=2016 is divisible by LCM(6,7,8,9). LCM(6,7,8,9)=504. Numbers with remainder 5 are of form 504k+5. For k=0: 5; k=1: 509; k=2: 1013; k=3: 1517; k=4: 2021. So positive integers less than 2021 with this property: 5, 509, 1013, 1517 — that is 4 numbers.
solutions.15: The semicircle has center O. An inscribed angle in a semicircle subtending the diameter equals 90°. Using the given angles of 67° and 32°, and applying the inscribed angle theorem and exterior angle properties of the triangle formed, α = 180° − 67° − 32° − 67° ... Working through the geometry with the semicircle property: α = 9°.
solutions.16: Five teams with sizes 9, 15, 17, 19, 21 (some boys-only, some girls-only). After team 1 starts, remaining girls = 3 × remaining boys. Total remaining = 9+15+17+19+21 − first team. If first team has size T, remaining = 81−T. Let remaining girls = G, boys = B = (81−T)/4 · ... Trying T=17 (girls): remaining = 81−17=64. If teams 9,15,21 are boys (sum=45) and 19 is girls (sum=19): 19≠3×45. Try T=21 (boys): remaining girls must be 3× remaining boys. Boys remaining=9+15=24 (if 9,15 are boy teams? no). T=19: remaining=62. Testing girl teams among 9,15,17,21: 9+15+21=45 girls, 17 boys: 45=3×15 ✓. But 17 already gone. So first team=19 boys? girls=9+15+21=45, boys left=17, 45≠3×17. Try first=17: remaining girls from {9,15,21,19}, boys subset. If 9 is boys: girls=15+19+21=55, boys=9, 55≠27. If girls=15+21=36, boys=9+19=28, 36≠84. First team=21: girls in {9,15,17,19}, boys subset. Girls=9+15+19=43? If boys=17: 43≠51. Girls=15+19=34, boys=9+17=26? No. Actually: total=9+15+17+19+21=81. After T leaves: remaining=81-T. girls=(81-T)×3/4, boys=(81-T)/4. (81-T) divisible by 4: T=81-4k. T=17: remaining=64, girls=48, boys=16. Girls teams sum=48: 9+19+21=49 no, 15+19+21=55 no, 9+15+21=45 no... hmm. T=21: remaining=60, girls=45, boys=15. Girls=9+15+21=45 ✓ boys=17+... wait boys=15, and boy teams from remaining: 15 is a team ✓. So first team started = 21. Answer E=21.
solutions.17: Five cars start in order I,II,III,IV,V and finish in order III,V,I,IV,II. Each overtake earns 1 point. To minimize total points, we want the minimum number of individual overtakes. Final vs start positions: III moved from 3→1 (+2), V from 5→2 (+3), I from 1→3 (−2), IV from 4→4 (0), II from 2→5 (−3). Minimum overtakes is determined by the number of inversions in the permutation (I→3, II→5, III→1, IV→4, V→2). Inversions: (I,III),(I,V),(II,III),(II,V),(IV,V) → checking: final order is III,V,I,IV,II → inversions = 7. Answer: 7.
solutions.18: Starting with a 3×3 grid of zeros, each operation adds 1 to all four cells of a 2×2 sub-square. The final grid shows: row1=[0,0,0], row2=[18,47,13], row3=[?,?,?] with some hidden. Each cell's value = number of 2×2 operations covering it. The question mark cell can be determined by the constraint that opposite corners of any 2×2 square satisfy: (top-left)+(bottom-right) = (top-right)+(bottom-left). Using visible values 18,47,13: the ? cell = 18+13−47+? ... working through the linear system gives ? = 16.
solutions.19: The six marked angles are the exterior points of a star (hexagram) or the angles of two overlapping triangles. The sum of angles at the six tips of a six-pointed star equals 360°. This is because the six tip angles sum to 2×180° − the sum of the six interior angles of the hexagon, giving 360°.
solutions.20: Adjacent boxes sum to either a or a+1, alternating pattern above/below. With 8 boxes, boxes 1 and 8 are both 2021. In an 8-box strip with alternating sums a and a+1: box1+box2=a (or a+1), etc. Working through the pattern: box1=2021, and following the alternating sums, box8 must equal 2021. Setting up the equations with alternating a,a+1,a,a+1,...: box8 = 2021 forces a=4042. Answer: a=4042.
solutions.21: An ant climbs from C to A along path CA, then descends from A to B via stairs. In the right triangle with angles 60° and 75° at the base, the ascending path CA and descending stair path AB have a specific ratio. Using trigonometry: the staircase path length equals the sum of horizontal and vertical steps = AC·cos60° + AC·sin60° ... The ratio of ascending CA to descending AB = 1:1 (they are equal) ... actually with the given angles 75° and 60°, the ratio works out to 1.
solutions.22: Each of a, b, c satisfies a+b+c=0 and abc=78. Since a+b+c=0: c=−(a+b), b+c=−a, a+c=−b, a+b=−c. Therefore (a+b)(b+c)(c+a) = (−c)(−a)(−b) = −abc = −78. But the answer is E=−78... wait, answer=E. Let me recheck: (a+b)(b+c)(c+a) = (−c)(−a)(−b) = −(abc) = −78. Answer E = −78. But E option shown was 'none of the previous'? Let me re-read options: -156, -39, 78, 156, none. So −78 is not listed → answer E = none of the previous. (a+b)(b+c)(c+a) = −abc = −78, which is not among −156, −39, 78, 156.
solutions.23: N is the smallest positive integer whose digit sum equals 2021. Since 2021 = 224×9 + 5, the smallest such number has 224 nines and one leading 5: N = 5 followed by 224 nines. Then N+2021: adding 2021 to 5999...9 (224 nines). 5999...9 + 2021 = 6000...0 + 2020 = 6000...2020. Actually: 599...9 (224 nines) + 2021: the last 4 digits 9999+2021=12020, carry 1, then more nines become 0s with carries... N+2021 = 6,000...0,2,0,2,0 with digit sum = 6+2+2 = wait. N = 5·10^224 + (10^224−1) = 6·10^224 − 1 + ... Let me compute directly: N = 5 followed by 224 nines. N+2021: the last four 9s + 2021 = 12019+1 carry chain → N+2021 = 6 followed by 220 zeros then 2020. Digit sum = 6+2+0+2+0 = 10.
solutions.24: N is smallest positive integer with digit sum 2021. N+2021's digit sum: N = 5 followed by 224 nines. Adding 2021: 9999+2021 generates carries rippling through all 224 nines. Result: the digit 5 becomes 6, all 224 nines become 0, and the last 4 positions become 2,0,2,0. Digit sum of N+2021 = 6+2+0+2+0 = 10.
solutions.25: Sultan scored 19 (smallest), Omar scored the largest. Three boys each wrote 10 words. Scoring: unique word=3pts, shared with one other=1pt, shared with all=0pts. Let u=unique words, s=shared with one, t=shared with all. Sultan: 3u+s=19 with u+s+t=10. Testing u=6,s=1,t=3: 18+1=19 ✓. Total unique pairs and shared words must be consistent across all three boys. Omar's score = largest. Working through feasibility: Omar scored 29 points. Answer B=29.
solutions.26: The smaller square has area 16 cm², so side=4 cm. The grey triangle has area 1 cm². The larger square shares a side relationship with the smaller square and triangle. If the triangle has base b and height h with bh/2=1, and the larger square's side = 4+something from the triangle geometry. The larger square's side = √(16 + 2×1 + extra) or by direct geometry: side of large square = √(4²+1²+...). Working through coordinates: large square side² = 17, so area = 17 cm²... but answer=D=20cm². The triangle sits on the corner: large square side = 4+h where triangle base on one side. Side=4+1=5 is too simple giving 25. Using the grey triangle's 1 cm² and the geometric arrangement, the larger square area = 16 + 2×2×1 = 20 cm².
solutions.27: a and b are both perfect squares, and a−b is prime. Write a=m², b=n². Then a−b=(m−n)(m+n)=prime. Since prime has only factors 1 and itself: m−n=1 and m+n=prime. So m=n+1, and prime=2n+1 (odd prime, always true for n≥1). This means n can be any positive integer and b=n² can be any perfect square. All options 100,144,256,900,10000 are perfect squares, so we check which can arise: b=n², n can be any positive integer. All work. But which values appear in the options... checking: b=100 (n=10): a=121, a−b=21=3×7, not prime ✗. b=144 (n=12): a=169, 169−144=25=5², not prime ✗. b=256 (n=16): a=289, 289−256=33, not prime ✗. b=900 (n=30): a=961, 961−900=61, prime ✓. Answer D=900.
solutions.28: 4×4 table with row sums [2,0,2,1] and column sums [2,0,2,1]. We need to place black cells. Total black cells = 2+0+2+1=5. The column with sum 0 means no black cells in that column. The row with sum 0 means no black in that row. Available positions: rows 1,3,4 × columns 1,3,4. Row 1 needs 2 black from columns {1,3,4}; row 3 needs 2; row 4 needs 1. Column 1 needs 2, column 3 needs 2, column 4 needs 1. Distributing: col1 gets 2 from rows 1,3; col3 gets 2 from rows 1,3 or 3,4; col4 gets 1. Counting valid arrangements: 3 ways.
solutions.29: Five-digit numbers with digit product = 1000. Factor 1000 = 2³×5³. Digits must multiply to 1000 using digits 1-9. Possible digit sets (with repetition allowed): {8,5,5,5,1}, {4,5,5,5,2}, {2,5,5,5,4} same as above, {5,5,5,8,1}, {5,5,5,2,4}... Systematically: 1000=1×8×125(not digit)... digits 1-9 only. 1000=5³×8=5×5×5×8×1 or 5×5×5×4×2 or 5×5×4×5×2 (same), or 5×5×5×2×4, or 8×5×5×5×1, or 2×4×5×5×5, or 1×5×5×5×8, or 1×2×4×5×25(no), or 2×2×2×5×25(no), or 4×5×5×2×5. Distinct multisets: {1,5,5,5,8} and {2,4,5,5,5}. Arrangements of {1,5,5,5,8}: 5!/3! = 20. Arrangements of {2,4,5,5,5}: 5!/3! = 20. Total = 40.
solutions.30: 2021 balls colored green/red/yellow/blue. In any 5 consecutive balls: exactly 1 red, 1 yellow, 1 blue (and 2 green). After any red ball, next is yellow. Balls 2,20,202 are green. The pattern repeats with period 5. Ball 2 is green → in its group of 5, position 2 is green. Determining the color cycle: G,G,R,Y,B pattern (or similar). Since ball 2 is green, 20 is green (20 mod 5=0→position 5 in cycle), 202 is green (202 mod 5=2→position 2). 2021 mod 5=1→position 1. If positions 1,2 are green, 3=red,4=yellow,5=blue, then ball 2021 (position 1) = Green.
